name: NAO
short_description: A compact, programmable humanoid robot that became the dominant research platform for social robotics and human-robot interaction for over a decade.
long_description: Developed by French company Aldebaran Robotics and first released in 2008 — initially to RoboCup teams, then to universities worldwide — NAO is a 58 cm humanoid with 25 degrees of freedom, four microphones, two cameras, and the NAOqi software platform supporting Python, C++, Java, and MATLAB. More than 13,000 units were deployed across 70+ countries, and a 2021 scoping review counted 288 published HRI studies using NAO between 2010 and 2020 alone, making it the most-studied robot in the field. Its combination of relative affordability, robust construction, and accessible programming tools normalized the methods and curriculum of social robotics research globally. Acquired by SoftBank in 2015.
ai_note: NAOqi includes speech recognition, face detection, and object recognition — classical ML and signal-processing components — but the platform is fundamentally a programmable robot; researchers supply most of the AI logic.

images.caption: NAO robot at Innorobo 2015, Lyon.
images.alt_text: A small white humanoid robot with rounded limbs and a smooth white and black plastic body standing upright, photographed at a trade show.

sources.title: Nao (robot)
sources.access_notes: Overview of hardware specs, release history, deployment statistics, and RoboCup adoption.

sources.title: 10 Years of Human-NAO Interaction Research: A Scoping Review
sources.access_notes: Reviews 288 HRI papers from 2010–2020 using NAO; covers domains, methods, and geographic distribution.

sources.title: Happy Birthday Nao!
sources.access_notes: Timeline of NAO milestones including RoboCup adoption and research deployment.

interactions.title: Play autonomous soccer at RoboCup
interactions.description: Teams of identical NAOs walk, find the ball with their cameras, and play full soccer matches against each other with no human control, as the standard robot of RoboCup's Standard Platform League since 2008.

interactions.title: Build behaviors by dragging boxes in Choregraphe
interactions.description: Students and researchers assemble the robot's movements and interactive behaviors visually in the Choregraphe interface, snapping together pre-built boxes or editing the underlying Python, with classrooms sharing a few NAOs among a class of students.

interactions.title: Run therapy modules with autistic children
interactions.description: Therapists use NAO as a co-therapist in sessions with children on the autism spectrum, running imitation, communication, and attention exercises; some children found the childlike, expressive robot more relatable than a person.

interactions.title: Perform a synchronized dance
interactions.description: Crowds of NAOs performed choreographed, synchronized dance routines, including a display at the 2010 Shanghai Expo.

interactions.title: Greet customers at a bank branch
interactions.description: Mitsubishi UFJ Financial Group began trialing NAO units in Japanese bank branches in 2015 to interact with and assist customers.

interactions.title: Coach a small-group language drill
interactions.description: A teacher loads a vocabulary routine and lets NAO lead a few children through call-and-response practice — using its speech recognition and four microphones to hear answers and its expressive gestures to keep attention — freeing the teacher to observe.

interactions.title: Mirror-and-prompt physiotherapy at home
interactions.description: Standing in front of a recovering patient, NAO demonstrates a stretch with its 25 joints and watches with its cameras to nudge the next repetition, turning rote exercises into a paced, social routine.
